topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical weights pressing down on Elias’s chest. He stood before the canvas, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the frame. The vibrant chaos he had poured onto the linen—a storm of bruised purples and sharp, jagged lines—was now bathed in the cool, indifferent glow of public scrutiny. A low, tight knot formed just beneath his sternum, a familiar, unwelcome tightening. He watched a patron drift past, their laughter sharp and bright, completely oblivious to the silent tension radiating off him. *They don't see the struggle,* he thought, his jaw clenching so hard it ached. He wanted to retreat, to dissolve into the shadows of his studio, but he was anchored here, a captive audience to his own creation, displayed without his consent.

[story 2]

"It’s… loud," Clara murmured, her voice thin and reedy as she circled the installation. She didn't look at the artwork itself, but at the surrounding crowd, her eyes darting nervously. I kept my gaze fixed on the floorboards, tracing the pattern of dust motes dancing in the shafts of weak afternoon light slicing through the high windows. Every appreciative murmur felt like a tiny pinprick against my skin. My hands, usually steady enough to coax life from clay, were trembling slightly as I adjusted my collar. The feeling in my gut was heavy, like submerged stones. I wanted to vanish, to become invisible, to erase the very space I occupied, but the silence of my own anxiety was deafening in the crowded room.

[story 3]

He traced the outline of the sculpture with a fingertip, the cool marble offering no comfort. The attention was a physical assault, a relentless tide washing over him from every direction. His shoulders were hunched inward, a self-imposed fortress against the warmth of the room. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped him, ragged and shallow. He felt a profound sense of isolation, as if the very air around the piece was vibrating with judgment. *They think they own the shape of my despair,* he mused, his voice barely a rasp when he finally spoke to a nearby critic, his tone flat, devoid of any warmth. He simply nodded, a gesture of profound resignation, and turned his back on the spectacle.
